teaching.title: What is OOP?
teaching.analogy: Object-Oriented Programming is like an architect designing buildings. The CLASS is the blueprint — it defines what a building looks like and what it can do. Each actual BUILDING constructed from that blueprint is an OBJECT (instance). All buildings from the same blueprint share the same structure, but each has its own address and unique features.
teaching.content: OOP groups data (attributes) and behaviors (methods) into a single unit called a CLASS. You create INSTANCES (objects) from classes. This models real-world things naturally — a Dog class, a Car class, a Player class.

examples.title: Defining a Class
examples.explanation: class keyword, CamelCase name, body indented.
examples.code:
class Dog:
    # Class body goes here
    pass  # placeholder

# Creating instances (objects):
my_dog = Dog()     # first dog object
your_dog = Dog()   # second dog object

print(type(my_dog))   # <class '__main__.Dog'>
print(my_dog is your_dog)  # False — different objects!

examples.title: Attributes & the Dot Operator
examples.explanation: Access everything on an object with . (dot).
examples.code:
class Car:
    pass

my_car = Car()
my_car.color = 'red'    # add attribute
my_car.speed = 120

print(my_car.color)     # red
print(my_car.speed)     # 120

teaching.funFact: Python is EVERYTHING objects! Integers, strings, lists — they're all objects from built-in classes. int, str, list are classes. That's why 'hello'.upper() works — upper() is a method of the str class! 🤯

teaching.title: __init__ — The Constructor
teaching.analogy: __init__ is the birth certificate of every object. The moment an object is created (born), __init__ runs automatically and sets its starting values — name, health, score, position — whatever it needs to begin life.
teaching.content: __init__ is a special method (dunder = double underscore) called automatically when you create an object. It initializes the object's attributes. 'self' refers to the specific object being created.

examples.title: __init__ in Action
examples.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name  # instance attribute
        self.age = age

    def bark(self):
        print(f'{self.name} says: Woof!')

rex = Dog('Rex', 3)
bella = Dog('Bella', 5)

print(rex.name)   # Rex
print(bella.age)  # 5
rex.bark()        # Rex says: Woof!

examples.title: self is the object itself
examples.explanation: self.name belongs to THAT specific instance.
examples.code:
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name, hp=100):
        self.name = name
        self.hp = hp
        self.level = 1  # default

p1 = Player('Neo')
p2 = Player('Trinity', hp=80)

p1.hp -= 30
print(p1.hp)   # 70
print(p2.hp)   # 80 (unaffected!)

teaching.funFact: __init__ is Python's constructor, but Python also has __del__ (destructor), __str__ (string representation), __len__, __add__, and 80+ more 'dunder' methods. They make your classes feel like built-in types! 🎩

mcq.q: What keyword defines a class in Python?
mcq.explanation: Class Definition: 'class ClassName:' creates a new class. Python convention: class names use PascalCase (CapWords). The colon and indentation define the class body.

mcq.q: What is 'self' in a class method?
options: The current instance (object) the method is called on
mcq.explanation: self Intel: 'self' is a reference to the specific INSTANCE the method is being called on. When you call rex.bark(), Python passes rex as self automatically. Every instance method needs self as first parameter.

mcq.q: When does __init__ run?
options: Automatically when an object is created
mcq.explanation: Constructor: __init__ runs AUTOMATICALLY every time you create a new object with ClassName(). It initializes the object's starting state. You don't call __init__ directly.

mcq.q: dog1 = Dog('Rex'). dog2 = Dog('Bella'). Are dog1 and dog2 the same object?
options: No — they are two separate instances
mcq.explanation: Separate Instances: Each call to Dog() creates a DIFFERENT object in memory. They share the same class blueprint, but changes to dog1.hp don't affect dog2.hp — they're independent!

mcq.q: What is the naming convention for class names in Python?
mcq.explanation: Convention: Python class names use PascalCase (CapitalizedWords): Dog, PlayerScore, BankAccount. Variables and functions use snake_case. This mirrors Java's class naming.

coding.title: Class Keyword
coding.objective: What keyword creates a class definition?
coding.hint: 5 letters — same as Java, C++, and most OOP languages.

coding.title: Constructor Name
coding.objective: What is the special method that auto-runs on object creation?

coding.title: Self Reference
coding.objective: After 'p = Player('Neo')', what does p.name return?
coding.hint: __init__ stores 'Neo' in self.name. p.name reads it back.

options: Missing 'self' as first parameter: def __init__(self, name, age)
debugging.answer: Missing 'self' as first parameter: def __init__(self, name, age)
debugging.hint: Every instance method MUST have 'self' as the first parameter. It's how Python passes the object reference.

options: Must use self.color = color to store as instance attribute
color = color is a valid swap
debugging.answer: Must use self.color = color to store as instance attribute
debugging.hint: 'color = color' just reassigns the local parameter to itself. Use 'self.color = color' to attach it to the object.

prediction.explanation: Each increment() call adds 1 to self.count. Called 3 times: 0→1→2→3. Output: 3.
a and b are two DIFFERENT objects in memory. == on objects compares identity by default (not values). They're at different memory addresses → False.

teaching.title: Instance Methods & __str__
teaching.analogy: Instance methods are the VERBS of an object — what it can DO. A Dog can bark(). A Player can attack(). __str__ is the object's name tag — when Python needs to display the object as text, it calls __str__ automatically.
teaching.content: Instance methods are functions defined inside a class that take self as the first parameter. __str__ is a special method returning a string representation — called automatically by print() and str().

examples.title: Methods in Action
examples.explanation: Methods access and modify the object's own data via self.

teaching.funFact: __repr__ is another dunder method — it returns an 'official' string representation for debugging. When you type an object in the Python shell, __repr__ is called, not __str__! 🔍

teaching.title: Inheritance — Child Classes
teaching.analogy: Inheritance is genetic — a child inherits traits from the parent. In OOP, a child class (subclass) automatically gets all the methods and attributes of the parent class. It can also OVERRIDE them with its own version, or ADD new ones.
teaching.content: class Child(Parent) creates a subclass. The child inherits all parent methods automatically. super() calls the parent's version of a method. Override a method by defining it again in the child.

examples.title: Inheritance in Action
examples.explanation: Child gets everything from Parent. super() calls parent's __init__.

examples.title: super() — Use Parent's Code

teaching.funFact: Python supports MULTIPLE inheritance — a class can inherit from multiple parents! class Duck(Flying, Swimming). This is rare and can get complex (solved by Python's MRO — Method Resolution Order). 🦆

mcq.q: What is a 'method' in a class?
options: A function defined inside a class
mcq.explanation: Method: A method is a function defined inside a class. It has access to the object's data via 'self'. Methods define the object's behaviors — what it can DO.

mcq.q: What does __str__ return?
options: A string representation of the object, shown when you print() it
mcq.explanation: __str__: This dunder (double underscore) method returns a string. Python calls it automatically when you use print(obj) or str(obj). Define it to give your objects human-readable descriptions.

mcq.q: How do you create a class Dog that inherits from Animal?
mcq.explanation: Inheritance Syntax: class ChildClass(ParentClass): is Python's inheritance syntax. The parent class name goes in parentheses. Different from Java (extends) and C++ (: public Parent).

mcq.q: What does super().__init__() do in a child class?
mcq.explanation: super(): super().__init__() explicitly calls the parent class's constructor. This ensures the parent's attributes (self.name, etc.) are initialized before the child adds its own. Essential in inheritance chains.

mcq.q: If a child class defines the same method as its parent, what happens?
options: The child's version overrides (replaces) the parent's
mcq.explanation: Method Override: Defining a method with the same name in a child class REPLACES the parent's version for that child. This is called 'overriding'. Call super().method() if you want to also run the parent's code.

coding.title: Inheritance Syntax
coding.objective: How do you make Dog inherit from Animal? Fill in the class line.
coding.hint: class ChildName(ParentName): — the parent goes in parentheses.

coding.title: super() Call
coding.objective: How do you call the parent's __init__ from inside a child class?
coding.hint: super() gives access to the parent class.

coding.title: __str__ Method
coding.objective: What method should you define for print(obj) to show a custom string?
coding.hint: The double-underscore string method — called automatically by print().

options: Missing super().__init__(name) — parent's name attribute is never initialized
debugging.answer: Missing super().__init__(name) — parent's name attribute is never initialized
debugging.hint: Without super().__init__(name), the parent class never sets self.name. Always call super().__init__() in child constructors.

options: Method not called — missing parentheses: c.speak() not c.speak
debugging.answer: Method not called — missing parentheses: c.speak() not c.speak
debugging.hint: c.speak is a reference to the method. c.speak() actually CALLS it. Without (), you print the method object, not its result.

prediction.explanation: Square overrides area() from Shape. Square.area() returns self.side ** 2 = 4² = 16.
B inherits from A and doesn't override hello(). Python finds hello() in the parent class A and calls it. Output: 'A'.

teaching.title: Python Modules — Built-in Libraries
teaching.analogy: Python modules are like toolboxes. The Python toolshed has hundreds of pre-built toolboxes (math, random, os, datetime, json). Instead of building a hammer from scratch, you import the toolbox and use its hammer. 'import math' unlocks the entire math toolbox.
teaching.content: A module is a file containing Python code. Python ships with 200+ standard library modules. Use 'import module_name' to load one. Access tools with module.function(). Import specific items with 'from module import item'.

examples.title: Common Standard Modules
examples.explanation: The Python standard library covers most needs.
examples.code:
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))    # 4.0
print(math.pi)          # 3.14159...
print(math.ceil(4.2))   # 5

import random
print(random.randint(1, 6))   # dice roll!
print(random.choice(['a','b','c']))

import datetime
now = datetime.datetime.now()
print(now.year, now.month, now.day)

examples.title: Import Styles
examples.code:
# Style 1: import module (must use module.name)
import math
math.sqrt(25)

# Style 2: from module import name (use directly)
from math import sqrt, pi
sqrt(25)  # no math. prefix
print(pi)

# Style 3: import as alias (rename)
import datetime as dt
dt.datetime.now()

teaching.funFact: Python's standard library is so comprehensive that the Python community calls it 'batteries included'. You can do web requests (urllib), parse JSON, zip files, send emails, and encrypt data — all without installing anything extra! 🔋

teaching.title: Creating Your Own Modules
teaching.analogy: Your own module is a custom toolbox YOU build. Save code in 'tools.py'. Then 'import tools' in another file to use it. You've just created a reusable library for your own projects!
teaching.content: Any .py file is a module. Save functions/classes in mymodule.py and import them with 'import mymodule'. The __name__ == '__main__' guard prevents code from running on import.

examples.title: Creating mymath.py
examples.explanation: Any .py file can be a module.
examples.code:
# mymath.py
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

PI = 3.14159

# main.py (in same directory)
import mymath
print(mymath.add(5, 3))        # 8
print(mymath.multiply(4, 4))   # 16
print(mymath.PI)               # 3.14159

examples.title: __name__ == '__main__' Guard
examples.explanation: Prevents test code from running when imported.
examples.code:
# mymodule.py
def greet(name):
    return f'Hello, {name}!'

# This block only runs when mymodule.py
# is executed directly — NOT when imported:
if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(greet('World'))  # test code
    print('Module test ran')

teaching.funFact: pip (Python Package Installer) lets you install 500,000+ third-party modules from PyPI. 'pip install requests' installs a module for HTTP requests. 'pip install pandas' installs a data analysis powerhouse! 🌐

mcq.q: What keyword loads a Python module?
mcq.explanation: Import Keyword: 'import module_name' loads the module and makes all its contents available. You then access them with module_name.function() syntax.

mcq.q: What does 'from math import sqrt' let you do?
mcq.explanation: from import: 'from math import sqrt' imports ONLY sqrt into your current namespace. You can then call sqrt(16) directly instead of math.sqrt(16). Cleaner but risks name clashes.

mcq.q: What is 'import numpy as np' doing?
options: Importing numpy and aliasing it to 'np' for shorter code
mcq.explanation: Alias: 'as np' creates a short alias. Instead of numpy.array(), you write np.array(). This is convention for popular libraries: numpy→np, pandas→pd, matplotlib→plt.

mcq.q: What does if __name__ == '__main__': do?
options: Runs code ONLY when that file is executed directly, not when imported
mcq.explanation: __name__: When a file runs directly, __name__ is '__main__'. When imported, __name__ is the module's filename. This guard prevents test code from running accidentally on import.

mcq.q: import random. Which generates a random integer from 1 to 10?
mcq.explanation: random.randint(a,b): Returns a random integer N such that a <= N <= b. Note: BOTH endpoints are INCLUDED — unlike range() which excludes the end!

coding.title: Import a Module
coding.objective: How do you import the math module?

coding.title: Square Root
coding.objective: Using 'import math', what does math.sqrt(25) return?

coding.title: Random Number
coding.objective: What function generates a random int between 1 and 6 (inclusive)?

options: After 'import math', must use math.sqrt(16) — or change to 'from math import sqrt'
debugging.answer: After 'import math', must use math.sqrt(16) — or change to 'from math import sqrt'
debugging.hint: 'import math' doesn't bring sqrt into your namespace. Use math.sqrt() or 'from math import sqrt'.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug:

from random import *
randint(1, 10)  # works but...
# Now 'choice', 'random', 'shuffle' etc. all pollute namespace
options: 'import *' imports EVERYTHING and can cause name clashes. Import specific names instead: from random import randint
debugging.answer: 'import *' imports EVERYTHING and can cause name clashes. Import specific names instead: from random import randint
debugging.hint: 'import *' is bad practice — it pollutes the namespace and makes code hard to read (you can't tell where names come from).

prediction.explanation: math.ceil(3.1) rounds UP to 4. math.floor(3.9) rounds DOWN to 3.
round(value, ndigits) rounds to ndigits decimal places. pi ≈ 3.14159... rounded to 2 places = 3.14.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE ARCHITECT
teaching.analogy: The Architect designed the Code Clash system itself using OOP, inheritance, and Python modules. To defeat it, prove you understand how to DESIGN real software — not just write one-off scripts!
teaching.content: THE ARCHITECT is the final boss of the Python track. It tests classes, __init__, self, instance methods, __str__, inheritance, super(), method overriding, module imports, and the standard library. Win to earn the 'Python Master' badge!

examples.title: Full Python mastery
examples.code:
✅ class, __init__, self, instances
✅ Instance methods, __str__ dunder
✅ Inheritance: class Child(Parent)
✅ super().__init__() 
✅ Method overriding
✅ import, from import, as alias
✅ math, random, datetime modules
✅ Custom modules & __name__

teaching.funFact: The entire Python standard library is written IN Python (mostly). This means you can read the source code of any standard module: find it in your Python installation and open it in a text editor! 🔓

mcq.q: class Dog(Animal): What does Dog inherit?
options: ALL methods and attributes from Animal
mcq.explanation: Full Inheritance: The child class gets ALL methods and attributes from the parent automatically. Dog doesn't re-define Animal's methods — it just uses them unless it overrides them.

mcq.q: What does print(obj) display when __str__ is defined?
mcq.explanation: __str__: print() automatically calls __str__(). So print(Player('Neo')) could show: 'Player: Neo (Lv.1)' instead of '<__main__.Player object at 0x...>'.

mcq.q: from math import sqrt. Now can you call sqrt(16) directly?
options: Yes — 'from import' brings it into local scope
mcq.explanation: from import: 'from math import sqrt' imports sqrt directly into your namespace. You call sqrt(16) without the 'math.' prefix. Useful for frequently used functions.

mcq.q:
What is the output?
class A:
    def greet(self): return 'Hi from A'
class B(A):
    def greet(self): return 'Hi from B'
b = B()
print(b.greet())
mcq.explanation: Method Override: B defines its own greet() — this OVERRIDES A's version for B instances. b.greet() calls B's version → 'Hi from B'.

mcq.q: What does math.sqrt() always return?
mcq.explanation: Float Return: math.sqrt() always returns a float, even for perfect squares. math.sqrt(9) → 3.0 (not 3). Use int(math.sqrt(9)) or ** 0.5 if you need an integer.

coding.title: Boss: Complete the Class
coding.objective: def __init__(self, name, hp): What stores the hp value as an instance attribute?
coding.hint: self refers to the current instance — use it to store instance data.

coding.title: Boss: Module Use
coding.objective: Using import random, how do you pick a random item from a list?
coding.starterCode:
import random
weapons = ['sword', 'bow', 'staff']
pick = random.___(weapons)
print(pick)
coding.hint: random.choice() picks one random element from a sequence.

debugging.description:
🚨 BOSS BUG:

class Warrior(Hero):
    def __init__(self, name, hp, weapon):
        self.weapon = weapon
        # Missing super().__init__() !

w = Warrior('Neo', 100, 'sword')
print(w.name)  # AttributeError: has no attribute 'name'
options: Missing super().__init__(name, hp) — parent's attributes (name, hp) are never set
debugging.answer: Missing super().__init__(name, hp) — parent's attributes (name, hp) are never set
debugging.hint: Always call super().__init__() at the start of a child's __init__ to initialize the parent's attributes.

prediction.explanation: Area = π × r² = π × 9 ≈ 28.274... rounded to 2 decimal places = 28.27.
